note: Long-input stress case for the wasm parity gate. Tokenizes to 429 WordPiece tokens, above the model's catalog max_input_tokens() advisory value of 256 and below the BERT position table cap of 512, so it exercises truncation-boundary-adjacent behavior without actually hitting the position-table limit. No HF golden embedding is recorded here: this fixture backs a wasm-vs-native self-consistency check (see embed_parity_wasm.mjs), not a wasm-vs-HF check.
input: Modern transformer inference engines increasingly favor compact, dependency light runtimes over heavyweight frameworks bundled with dozens of transitive libraries. A pure systems language implementation can load model weights directly from a safetensors file, parse the accompanying configuration, build a tokenizer from its serialized vocabulary, and execute every matrix multiplication, normalization, and attention computation without ever shelling out to an interpreted runtime. This approach trades some flexibility for predictability: memory usage stays bounded and easy to reason about, startup latency drops because there is no virtual machine to warm up, and the resulting binary can be embedded in constrained environments such as browsers, edge devices, or serverless functions where installing a full machine learning stack is impractical or simply not permitted. Embedding models in particular benefit from this design because their computational graph is comparatively small and shallow: a handful of encoder layers, a pooling operation, and a normalization step are usually enough to turn a sentence into a dense vector suitable for semantic search, clustering, or retrieval augmented generation. When that same code path is compiled to a portable bytecode format and shipped to a JavaScript host, the interesting engineering question becomes whether the numerical behavior survives the trip unchanged. Floating point arithmetic is not always bit for bit identical across compilation targets, math library implementations can differ subtly between a native host and a sandboxed virtual machine, and any divergence in how the tokenizer counts, truncates, or pads a long input can silently change the pooled representation even when every matrix multiply agrees to the last decimal place. Proving that a compiled artifact produces the same answer as its native sibling therefore requires more than a single short sentence; it requires exercising the exact code paths that only manifest once an input grows past a model's ordinary length, because truncation boundaries are precisely where two otherwise equivalent implementations are most likely to quietly disagree with each other, and where a regression that appears nowhere on short inputs can hide undetected for a very long time before someone finally notices the drift in production and has to work backward to find the root cause.
